status: v0.1-expansion-in-progress; new rows review_status:proposed pending clinician sign-off
notes: Facts/thresholds only; no guideline prose copied. `in_registry:false` sources are disclosed citation gaps to backfill. See README.md for the schema.

sources.label: WHO Expert Consultation — appropriate BMI for Asian populations (Lancet 2004)

rows.overclaim_guard: Lower cutoffs reflect earlier metabolic risk, not a different disease; do not restate as 'South Asians are unhealthy at normal weight'.
rows.notes: Encoded in loinc.yaml as PRAJNA-BMI ref_high 23. Source in evidence registry as who-asian-bmi-2004 (grade A, approved). obese_at corrected 25->27.5 (WHO 2004 Asian high-risk action point; 2026-07-11).

sources.label: Diagnosis and Classification of Diabetes: Standards of Care in Diabetes—2024
Optimum BMI cut points to screen Asian Americans for type 2 diabetes (Araneta/Kanaya, Diabetes Care 2015)

rows.overclaim_guard: A screening trigger is not a diagnosis; a positive trigger means 'test glucose', not 'has diabetes'.
rows.notes: SouthAsianScreeningPack.diabetesScreenBMI23 (v2024.2), fires from age 30.

sources.label: ADA Standards of Care 2024
Araneta/Kanaya, Diabetes Care 2015

rows.notes: South Asians develop dysglycaemia ~a decade earlier; screen earlier.

sources.label: IDF consensus worldwide definition of the metabolic syndrome — ethnic-specific waist cutoffs

rows.notes: loinc.yaml 56115-9 ref_high 90 encodes the male SA cut.

sources.label: IDF consensus worldwide definition of the metabolic syndrome

rows.notes: SouthAsianScreeningPack.isCentralObesity female cut = 80 cm.

sources.label: Comparison of obesity indices for CVD risk classification in South Asian adults — The CARRS Study (Patel et al., PLoS One 2017)

rows.overclaim_guard: WtHR >=0.5 is a general adiposity rule; the South-Asian-specific claim is that it outperforms BMI here, not a distinct cutoff.
rows.notes: Used in SouthAsianScreeningPack.isCentralObesity as a fallback when waist+height on file. Source in evidence registry as carrs-patel-2017 (review_status: proposed — awaiting clinician sign-off).

sources.label: IDF metabolic syndrome consensus (2006)
Prevention of ASCVD in South Asians — NLA clinical perspective (2021)

rows.notes: IDF sex-specific low-HDL cut; women flagged at <50.

sources.label: IDF metabolic syndrome consensus (2006)

rows.overclaim_guard: Male cut equals the general threshold; the SA-specific piece is the female cut and the pattern's weight, not this value.
rows.notes: Included for completeness so the male/female contrast is explicit.

sources.label: AHA Scientific Statement — ASCVD in South Asians (Volgman 2018)
Lipid Association of India SA lipid targets (Enas 2013)
Risk factors for early MI in South Asians (INTERHEART, JAMA 2007)

rows.overclaim_guard: A normal LDL-C does not rule out risk when TG is high and HDL low; do not present LDL-C as the sole lipid target for SA users.
rows.notes: The SA-characteristic pattern (small-dense LDL, high ApoB/ApoA-I) that drives risk at normal LDL-C. Component values match general thresholds; the differentiation is the emphasis.

sources.label: NLA — Prevention of ASCVD in South Asians (2021)
AHA Volgman 2018

rows.overclaim_guard: The range is not SA-specific; the SA-specific claim is preference of ApoB over LDL-C, not a different cutoff.
rows.notes: SouthAsianScreeningPack.apobPreferred nudges ApoB when a lipid panel exists but ApoB doesn't.

sources.label: NLA 2024 Lp(a) focused update / AHA 2024 Lp(a) toolkit
2018 AHA/ACC/Multi-Society Blood Cholesterol Guideline

rows.overclaim_guard: Do NOT claim South Asians universally run high Lp(a): MASALA (Huffman 2019) found no Lp(a)-atherosclerosis signal within the cohort. The rationale is 'genetically fixed + test-once is cheap', plus higher elevated-prevalence, not a proven SA outcome association.
rows.notes: The 'test once' guidance is guideline-endorsed and not SA-specific; it appears here because it is under-done in this population. loinc.yaml Lp(a) mass ref_high 30 mg/dL, molar 43583-7 ref_high 75 nmol/L.

sa_value.note: LAI recommends more aggressive non-HDL targets by risk stratum

sources.label: Lipid Association of India SA lipid targets (Enas 2013)

rows.overclaim_guard: LAI targets are risk-stratified; do not present a single non-HDL cutoff as applying to all SA users.
rows.notes: Default non-HDL ref_high 130 from loinc.yaml; SA-specific aggressiveness is stratum-dependent — represented qualitatively pending clinician review.

sources.label: Isolated HbA1c identifies a different T2D subgroup vs glucose in Asian Indians — CARRS & MASALA (Gujral 2019)
ADA Standards of Care 2024

rows.overclaim_guard: HbA1c is not universally 'wrong' in SA users; the claim is that it can under- or over-read (iron deficiency, glycation rate), so borderline values warrant glucose confirmation.
rows.notes: The reference range is not changed; this is an interpretation flag on borderline values.

rows.analyte: Coronary artery calcium — baseline discussion age

sources.label: AHA Scientific Statement — ASCVD in South Asians (Volgman 2018)

rows.notes: SA develop CAD ~10 years earlier; earlier baseline CAC discussion. In-app, CAC nudges tie to PREVENT 5-<20% band.

sources.label: Endocrine Society — Evaluation, Treatment, and Prevention of Vitamin D Deficiency (2011)

rows.overclaim_guard: The reference range is not SA-specific; the SA-relevant point is high deficiency prevalence, warranting screening — not a different normal range.
rows.notes: SouthAsianScreeningPack.vitaminDScreen prompts if not measured within a year.

sources.label: MASALA cardiometabolic profile / HOMA-IR vs MESA (Kanaya 2014)

rows.overclaim_guard: Explicitly NOT guideline-backed; present as an optional/exploratory marker, never as recommended screening.
rows.notes: In-app copy flags this as not guideline-backed. Good example of an honest study-derived row.

sa_value.guidance: do NOT apply a race/ancestry coefficient

sources.label: New Creatinine- and Cystatin C–Based Equations without Race (CKD-EPI 2021, Inker et al., NEJM)

rows.overclaim_guard: Do NOT ship an ancestry-adjusted eGFR. The 2021 refit removed the race term as a social-not-biological construct that delayed care. This row exists to instruct consumers to NOT stratify eGFR by ancestry.
rows.notes: The credibility showcase row: an adjustment the field is moving away from, included precisely to warn against it. Consistent with loinc.yaml keeping eGFR at real LOINC 33914-3 with no ancestry term.

sa_value.guidance: treat estimate as a floor; it underestimates SA risk

sources.label: Development and Validation of the AHA PREVENT Equations (Circulation 2024)

rows.overclaim_guard: There is no validated SA multiplier in PREVENT; do not invent one. State the limitation ('treat as a floor'), do not apply a made-up adjustment.
rows.notes: Contrast with the eGFR row: here we flag under-estimation but still refuse to fabricate an adjustment. loinc.yaml PRAJNA-PREVENT plain_language already says 'treat it as a floor'.

sources.label: 2018 AHA/ACC/Multi-Society Blood Cholesterol Guideline

rows.overclaim_guard: The 175 mg/dL cut is not SA-specific; the SA-relevant point is that atherogenic dyslipidemia (high TG + low HDL) reaches it often even at a normal LDL-C.
rows.notes: Completes the atherogenic-dyslipidemia composite inputs. TG LOINC 2571-8.

sources.label: 2018 AHA/ACC/Multi-Society Blood Cholesterol Guideline

rows.overclaim_guard: The >=2 mg/L cut is not SA-specific; SA users more often exceed it due to higher visceral adiposity at the same BMI.
rows.notes: hs-CRP LOINC 30522-7.

sa_value.guidance: The 2018 AHA/ACC guideline lists South Asian ancestry as a formal ASCVD risk-enhancing factor; use it to move borderline/intermediate-risk patients toward the treat decision.

sources.label: 2018 AHA/ACC/Multi-Society Blood Cholesterol Guideline

rows.overclaim_guard: A risk-enhancer is not a risk multiplier; it does not by itself diagnose disease or mandate a statin — it shifts the borderline decision.
rows.notes: Explicitly named in the 2018 AHA/ACC Cholesterol Guideline risk-enhancer list.

sa_value.guidance: LAI recommends more aggressive LDL-C/non-HDL targets for SA very-high-risk patients.

sources.label: Lipid Association of India SA lipid targets (Enas 2013)
NLA — Prevention of ASCVD in South Asians (2021)

rows.overclaim_guard: LAI targets are risk-stratified; do not present a single cutoff as applying to all SA users. LDL-C <70 matches general very-high-risk guidance; the SA-specific piece is the tighter non-HDL target.
rows.notes: Tightens the non-HDL-130 reference row for the very-high-risk stratum. review pending clinician sign-off.

sources.label: Multi-society Delphi consensus statement on new fatty liver disease nomenclature (MASLD) (Rinella et al., 2023)
Ectopic fat depots and coronary artery calcium in South Asians vs other ethnic groups (Garg et al., JAHA 2016)

rows.overclaim_guard: A lower screening BMI reflects earlier ectopic-fat accumulation, not a different disease; MASLD is diagnosed by imaging/enzymes, not by BMI alone.
rows.notes: SA carry the highest intrahepatic fat of MESA ethnic groups at lower BMI (Garg 2016). MASLD nomenclature (2023) uses BMI >=23 for Asian populations.

sa_value.guidance: Multiply the SCORE2 estimate by the ancestry-specific factor before applying risk thresholds.

sources.label: 2021 ESC Guidelines on cardiovascular disease prevention in clinical practice

rows.overclaim_guard: The multiplier applies to SCORE2 specifically (derived from QRISK3 data); do not transplant it onto PREVENT or the Pooled Cohort Equations, which have no validated SA multiplier.
rows.notes: The only major guideline with a numeric SA correction factor (2021 ESC, Class IIa LOE B). Companion to the PREVENT 'treat as a floor' caveat row.

rows.analyte: Coronary artery calcium (South Asian age/sex percentiles)

sa_value.guidance: Use age/sex-specific percentiles, not the raw 100 cutoff, for younger SA patients.

sources.label: South Asian coronary artery calcium age/sex percentiles — MASALA + DILWALE (Tasdighi et al., JACC Advances 2025)

rows.overclaim_guard: These are cohort percentiles (MASALA+DILWALE), not a guideline threshold; CAC=0 remains reassuring at age 50 (55% of men, 80% of women) but less so at 70.
rows.notes: First SA-specific CAC percentile table to exist (n=2,743, ASCVD-free). review pending clinician sign-off.

sources.label: Remnant cholesterol and incident coronary artery calcium in South Asians — MASALA (Manghis et al., J Clin Lipidol 2025)

rows.overclaim_guard: Explicitly not a guideline cutoff; the top MASALA quartile (>=~33 mg/dL) was associated with incident CAC, not CAC progression. Present as exploratory. Calculate as total cholesterol - HDL-C - LDL-C.
rows.notes: MASALA: Q4 vs Q1 OR 2.57 for incident CAC over 5y, independent of LDL-C. review pending clinician sign-off.

sa_value.guidance: Do NOT apply APOL1 G1/G2 kidney-risk stratification to South Asians — these variants are high-frequency only in West-African ancestry (<1% in non-Africans).

sources.label: APOL1 kidney-risk variants are West-African ancestry–specific (review)

rows.overclaim_guard: This row exists to instruct consumers NOT to use APOL1 in a South Asian risk model; including it would be a false-ancestry-attribution error.
rows.notes: Credibility-showcase 'do-not-apply' row, in the spirit of the deprecated eGFR row.

sa_value.guidance: Do NOT surface alcohol-flush / fast-metabolizer warnings for South Asians based on ALDH2*2 or ADH1B*2 — these are predominantly East Asian (low single-digit % in SA).

sources.label: ALDH2*2 / ADH1B*2 alcohol-metabolism variants are predominantly East Asian (review)

rows.overclaim_guard: This row exists to instruct consumers NOT to apply East-Asian alcohol-metabolism genetics to South Asians.
rows.notes: Credibility-showcase 'do-not-apply' row. South Asians more often carry the slower ADH1B*1.

rows.overclaim_guard: The reference range is not SA-specific; the SA-relevant point is high deficiency prevalence in vegetarian South Asians, warranting screening — not a different normal range.
rows.notes: Exact parallel to the vitamin-D screening-emphasis row. B12 LOINC 2132-9.

sources.label: Hyperhomocysteinemia & cobalamin (B12) deficiency in Asian Indians (Refsum et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2001)
Vitamin B12 deficiency & hyperhomocysteinemia in rural and urban Indians (Yajnik et al., JAPI 2006)

sa_value.guidance: Offer carrier screening in South Asian ancestry (higher β-thalassemia, HbE, HbD-Punjab prevalence), especially preconception; low MCV with normal iron warrants electrophoresis.

rows.overclaim_guard: A carrier trait is not a disease; screening informs reproductive counseling and explains a spuriously altered HbA1c — it is not a diagnosis.
rows.notes: Also the mechanism behind the HbA1c interpretation caveat. MCV LOINC 30428-7.

sources.label: ACOG Practice Bulletin No. 78: Hemoglobinopathies in Pregnancy (Obstet Gynecol 2007)

sa_value.guidance: A normal ALT does not exclude MASLD; in SA with metabolic risk, use imaging (ultrasound / hepatic-fat) rather than relying on enzymes.

rows.overclaim_guard: ALT is not universally unreliable; the point is that a normal value is insufficient to rule out fatty liver in a high-metabolic-risk SA profile.
rows.notes: Pairs with the BMI-23 MASLD screening trigger. ALT LOINC 1742-6.

sources.label: Proportion of NAFLD/MASLD patients with normal ALT: systematic review & meta-analysis (BMC Gastroenterol 2020)

sa_value.guidance: A mildly low WBC / absolute neutrophil count can be a normal ancestry-linked variant; do not over-investigate isolated mild neutropenia without other features.

rows.overclaim_guard: Benign ethnic neutropenia is best-established for African ancestry; the South Asian evidence base is thinner — flag it as a possibility, not a rule.
rows.notes: Included with an explicit evidence-strength caveat. WBC LOINC 6690-2.

sources.label: Benign ethnic neutropenia (Atallah-Yunes et al., Blood Rev 2019)

sa_value.action: In decreased-function carriers, limit simvastatin dose and prefer rosuvastatin or pravastatin.
sa_value.frequency_note: C-allele frequency in SA is broadly comparable to White populations (~15-20%).

rows.overclaim_guard: The recommendation is genotype-guided statin selection, not SA-specific dosing; there is no SA-specific dosing table.
rows.notes: CPIC 2022 statin/SLCO1B1 guideline. Default move in SA with statin myalgia: consider genotyping and switch off simvastatin before declaring intolerance.

sources.label: CPIC Guideline for SLCO1B1/ABCG2/CYP2C9 & statin-associated musculoskeletal symptoms (Clin Pharmacol Ther 2022)

sa_value.adult_equivalent: overweight = adult BMI 23, obese = adult BMI 27 at age 18

rows.overclaim_guard: These are cardiometabolic-risk-adjusted centiles on IAP 2015 charts; under-5 growth still follows WHO international standards regardless of ethnicity.
rows.notes: Plot SA children on CDC and IAP charts; when CDC says normal but IAP says at-risk, treat as at-risk. IAP 2015 (Khadilkar).

sources.label: Revised IAP 2015 growth charts / BMI cut-offs for 5-18y Indian children (Khadilkar et al., Indian J Endocrinol Metab 2015)

rows.analyte: Diabetes risk at low BMI (lean-diabetes phenotype)

sa_value.guidance: Do not gate diabetes-risk screening on BMI>=25 — T2D at BMI<25 is 2-4x more common in Asian Indians. Screen from BMI 23 and use waist-to-height >=0.5.

rows.overclaim_guard: Lean diabetes reflects reduced β-cell function / 'thin-fat' body composition, not the absence of metabolic risk; a normal BMI is not reassurance.
rows.notes: INSPIRED study. Reinforces the BMI-23 diabetes-screening trigger.

sources.label: Young-onset diabetes in Asian Indians linked to lower beta-cell function (INSPIRED; Diabetologia 2022)

sa_value.early_screen: first-trimester screening for high-risk groups including South Asian ancestry
sa_value.guidance: SA women develop GDM at lower BMI and earlier in pregnancy; screen earlier, same thresholds.

rows.overclaim_guard: The diagnostic thresholds are unchanged; the SA-specific action is earlier screening, not a different cutoff.
rows.notes: IADPSG 2010; ADA 2024 §15 (Pregnancy); FIGO 2015.

sources.label: IADPSG recommendations on diagnosis & classification of hyperglycemia in pregnancy (Diabetes Care 2010)
Diagnosis and Classification of Diabetes: Standards of Care in Diabetes—2024

rows.analyte: Age at natural menopause (South Asian women)

sa_value.guidance: ~30% of US SA women experience early/premature menopause (<=45y) vs <7% in other US groups.

rows.overclaim_guard: Cohort estimate (n=405); earlier menopause raises cardiometabolic risk earlier but is not itself a disease.
rows.notes: Lyu, Climacteric 2025. New domain: women's health.

sources.label: Menopausal experience (hot flashes, urinary incontinence, mood) among South Asian American women — MASALA (Lyu et al., Climacteric 2025)

sa_value.effect_over_5y_vs_0_1: SBP +8.9 mmHg, DBP +4.1 mmHg, LDL-C +8.9 mg/dL

rows.overclaim_guard: Association from MASALA (n~415); parity is a risk-context flag, not a screening threshold.
rows.notes: Mehta, JACC Advances 2026. Surface on women's cardiometabolic panels.

sources.label: Association of Parity With ASCVD Risk Factors Among South Asian Women — MASALA (Mehta et al., JACC Advances 2026)

sa_value.guidance: Do NOT treat MTHFR C677T as a South Asian homocysteine variant — it is not enriched in SA. Elevated homocysteine here is driven by dietary (vegetarian) B12 deficiency; treat the B12, not the SNP.

rows.overclaim_guard: Credibility-showcase 'do-not-apply' row: the actionable target is B12 status, not MTHFR genotyping.
rows.notes: Driver evidence: Refsum 2001 (B12 deficiency → hyperhomocysteinemia in Indians).

sources.label: Hyperhomocysteinemia & cobalamin (B12) deficiency in Asian Indians (Refsum et al., Am J Clin Nutr 2001)

sa_value.note: The ApoB/ApoA-I ratio was the strongest single modifiable MI predictor in South Asians (INTERHEART; population-attributable risk ~47%).
sa_value.guidance: Where available, weight the ApoB/ApoA-I ratio over LDL-C alone for SA risk discussions.

rows.overclaim_guard: No SA-specific numeric ratio cutoff is defined here; the claim is the ratio's predictive weight, not a threshold.
rows.notes: Reuses the INTERHEART citation already in the dataset. PRAJNA-APOBA1.

sources.label: Risk factors for early MI in South Asians (INTERHEART, JAMA 2007)

sa_value.guidance: Reference range unchanged; the SA-relevant point is high hypothyroidism prevalence in Indian adults, supporting a lower threshold to check TSH.

rows.overclaim_guard: Not a redefined range — a prevalence-driven screening emphasis; do not present a different TSH normal range for SA.
rows.notes: Unnikrishnan, Indian thyroid epidemiology. Soft/borderline candidate. TSH LOINC 3016-3.

sources.label: Prevalence of hypothyroidism in adults: epidemiological study in eight cities of India (Unnikrishnan et al., Indian J Endocrinol Metab 2013)
